narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on academic and background factors from past students. The model estimated a 67% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting those predicted to succeed right away. This decision hinged on a few key aspects of your profile that stood out as the strongest influences. Most notably, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2—meaning you placed in the lower part of your class that year, compared to the typical student's middle-of-the-pack average around the 5th or 6th decile—pushed the prediction heavily toward failure, signaling potential challenges in sustaining strong performance. Your LSAT score of 26, well below the dataset average of about 37, was the second-biggest factor pulling in the same direction, as lower scores like yours have historically correlated with bar struggles for many students. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.50—higher than the typical 3.21 average—worked in your favor, nudging the prediction toward passing and showing your capacity for solid academic work earlier on. While factors like your middle family income bracket and full-time student status align with the norm for most applicants, they didn't sway the outcome much here. Compared to typical students who get approved, your combination of a low Year 3 decile and LSAT created a risk profile that outweighed your GPA strength. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male pursuing law—many in similar backgrounds succeed with targeted improvements—but the model flagged these as the top risks. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, while strengthening your later academic deciles through additional coursework or grad school. Building a narrative around your upward GPA trend could also help in reapplying or appealing. With focused effort on these areas, you can shift future predictions in your favor and pursue your legal ambitions.
